content:
Two transactions. One row. Both succeeded.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Concert venue. 340 seats. 412 confirmed bookings for the same show.
Two users clicked “Book” 1.2 seconds apart. Both got confirmation emails. Both got charged. One seat. Two owners.
Code review comment on the PR: “Do we need SELECT FOR UPDATE here?" Reply: "Should be fine, Postgres is ACID."
Postgres is ACID. Your application protocol was not.
SELECT FOR UPDATE vs optimistic locking — we chose neither
Pessimistic locking — lock the row before check:
BEGIN;
SELECT available_seats FROM shows WHERE id = $1 FOR UPDATE;
UPDATE shows SET available_seats = available_seats - 1 WHERE id = $1;
COMMIT;Optimistic locking — version column, fail on conflict:
UPDATE shows
SET available_seats = available_seats - 1, version = version + 1
WHERE id = $1 AND version = $2 AND available_seats > 0
RETURNING *;What we shipped:
SELECT available_seats FROM shows WHERE id = $1;
-- application checks count > 0
UPDATE shows SET available_seats = available_seats - 1 WHERE id = $1;Classic check-then-act race. Two transactions read available_seats = 1. Both pass. Both decrement. Column ended at -72. Application displayed 0. Reality: 412 bookings, 340 seats.
SELECT show_id, count(*) AS bookings, max(s.available_seats)
FROM bookings b JOIN shows s ON s.id = b.show_id
WHERE b.show_id = 8847 GROUP BY show_id;
-- bookings: 412, available_seats: -7272 oversold paying customers without seats. Refund cost: $18,400. Venue threatened contract cancellation. Total incident cost ~$45,000 including legal settlement.
The fix — pessimistic lock, optimistic path, and constraints
Pessimistic with timeout:
BEGIN;
SET lock_timeout = '2s';
SELECT id, available_seats FROM shows WHERE id = $1 FOR UPDATE;
UPDATE shows SET available_seats = available_seats - 1 WHERE id = $1;
INSERT INTO bookings (...);
COMMIT;Load test: 400 concurrent bookings on 1 seat. 1 success, 399 lock timeout. Zero double-book.
Optimistic for customer path — better flash-sale UX:
UPDATE shows SET available_seats = available_seats - 1, version = version + 1
WHERE id = $1 AND version = $2 AND available_seats > 0 RETURNING *;Zero rows → retry or “sold out.” 1 success, 399 retries <50ms each in load test.
CHECK constraint we should have had from day one:
ALTER TABLE shows ADD CONSTRAINT seats_non_negative
CHECK (available_seats >= 0);Would have failed the second UPDATE. One oversell instead of 72. CI now blocks inventory schema without non-negative check.
SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED — wrong tool for seats. Second booker skips locked row, might interpret as "show not found." Do not copy queue patterns into inventory.
→ PostgreSQL War Room — FOR UPDATE vs SKIP LOCKED vs advisory locks. Section we read at 2 AM after counting 412 bookings.
Code review checklist — now mandatory for inventory
→ Read path and write path in same transaction? → FOR UPDATE or optimistic version column? → lock_timeout set? → CHECK constraint on quantity columns? → Load test with concurrent requests?
PR that caused this: +47 lines, no concurrency test. Test added:
@pytest.mark.concurrent
def test_booking_race(n_seats=1, n_threads=50):
show_id = create_show(available=n_seats)
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=n_threads) as ex:
results = list(ex.map(attempt_booking, [show_id]*n_threads))
assert sum(1 for r in results if r.success) == n_seatsFails without lock. Passes with optimistic or pessimistic. Runs in CI. Inventory PRs require concurrency test file in diff — blocking in GitHub.
SERIALIZABLE isolation — considered. Postgres SSI would work. 30% throughput drop on hot shows. Rejected. Optimistic locking sufficient at our contention level.
Admin path, legal exposure, and recovery
Admin “hold seat for VIP” uses advisory lock:
SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('show_' || $1::text));
SELECT available_seats FROM shows WHERE id = $1 FOR UPDATE;Releases on commit — no idle lock risk. Two locking strategies. One table. Documented in schema comments.
72 customers refunded. 340 got apology email with free upgrade. Venue kept contract. New contracts require database-level non-negative seat enforcement and load test proof before go-live.
One code review comment ignored. Most expensive comment we ever wrote.
Atomic decrement without explicit SELECT FOR UPDATE
Post-incident refactor — single statement:
UPDATE shows SET available_seats = available_seats - 1
WHERE id = $1 AND available_seats > 0
RETURNING id, available_seats;0 rows = sold out. No separate SELECT. No check-then-act window. Row-level lock held for duration of UPDATE only. Simpler than FOR UPDATE for single-seat decrement. Load test: identical correctness, 40% less lock wait time.
We kept optimistic version column for audit trail. Atomic UPDATE for hot path.
Venue contract and technical due diligence
New venue contracts require:
→ CHECK constraint on available_seats >= 0 → Concurrency integration test in CI → Load test report before first live show → Penalty clause negotiated to refund-only
Legal and engineering aligned for first time on database constraints. Constraint names in contract appendix. Silly until it saves $45,000.
Double booking detection — belated safety net
Nightly job:
SELECT show_id, count(*) AS bookings, s.available_seats
FROM bookings b
JOIN shows s ON s.id = b.show_id
GROUP BY show_id, s.available_seats
HAVING count(*) > (340 - s.available_seats);Would have caught oversell within 24 hours even if race recurred. Alarm now fires within 5 minutes via streaming aggregation on booking count vs capacity.
Flash sale load test — numbers that convinced leadership
400 concurrent threads, 1 seat:
→ No lock: 847 false successes before test killed → Pessimistic FOR UPDATE: 1 success, 399 timeouts → Optimistic version: 1 success, 399 fast retries → Atomic UPDATE RETURNING: 1 success, 399 zero-row responses
Leadership understood after seeing 847 vs 1. Engineering got CI budget for concurrency tests.
Refund line item in postmortem
$18,400 refunds. $8,000 venue settlement. $12,000 engineering recovery. $6,600 legal. Total ~$45,000. One ignored code review comment. Cheapest fix was free: add FOR UPDATE or version column.
Serializable isolation — when we would use it
Postgres SSI detects read-write conflicts without explicit locks. 30% throughput drop on our hot show benchmark. Rejected for customer path. Consider for low-volume high-value transactions — wire transfers, not concert tickets.
Know the menu: check-then-act, pessimistic, optimistic, atomic UPDATE, serializable. Pick for contention profile.
Integration test in CI — blocking merge
@pytest.mark.concurrent
def test_booking_race(n_seats=1, n_threads=50):
show_id = create_show(available=n_seats)
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=n_threads) as ex:
results = list(ex.map(attempt_booking, [show_id]*n_threads))
assert sum(1 for r in results if r.success) == n_seats50 lines. Runs on every inventory PR. Failed on check-then-act branch. Passed after optimistic locking merge.
Venue almost walked — relationship recovery
Contract renewal in Q4. Oversell incident nearly killed $2M/year deal. Apology tour: CTO on-site, free ticketing integration work, database constraint demo to venue IT. Trust rebuilt over 6 weeks. Technical fix took 2 days. Relationship fix took 2 months.
Negative seat count in monitoring
SELECT id, available_seats FROM shows WHERE available_seats < 0;Alert fires immediately now. Would have fired at -1, not -72. Belated safety net. CHECK constraint is prevention. Monitoring is detection. Need both.
Code review comment — verbatim
“Do we need SELECT FOR UPDATE here?” “Should be fine, Postgres is ACID.”
$45,000 reply. Comment now pinned in team wiki under “expensive sentences.” New engineers read it in onboarding. Cheaper than repeating the incident.
lock_timeout user experience
2 second lock timeout means user sees “try again” not infinite spinner. 399 of 400 concurrent bookers got fast failure in load test. Acceptable for flash sale. Better than 412 confirmations for 340 seats.
PostgreSQL War Room — lock section saved us
At 2 AM counting 412 bookings, we opened the lock contention chapter. FOR UPDATE vs SKIP LOCKED vs advisory locks — SKIP LOCKED wrong for seats, documented in bold. Almost shipped wrong hotfix. War room guide paid for itself before sunrise.
PostgreSQL War Room — lock patterns, emergency queries, when row count goes negative. Open it before you open Slack. 340 seats. 412 bookings. One ignored review comment. FOR UPDATE or version column — pick one before the next onsale. Postgres is ACID. Your protocol might not be. Load test with 50 concurrent threads before the next flash sale — not after the oversell incident teaches you the hard way.
→ 30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — oversell and double-booking case files with estimated revenue impact. Read one before your next flash sale.

Find your check-then-act pattern — SELECT, if, UPDATE. That is not atomic. That is not atomic. Postgres will not save you. FOR UPDATE or version column. Pick one.
PostgreSQL War Room — lock patterns, emergency queries, when the row count goes negative.
Froquiz runs concurrency scenarios in Senior Dev rounds — pick the locking strategy before 340 seats become 412 bookings.
→ Froquiz
